nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Camville
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: French; English and Irish (of Norman origin): habitational name from any of the places in Calvados and Seine-Maritime named Carville, from the Scandinavian personal name Kári + Old French ville ‘settlement’.
Irish: shortened Anglicized form of Ó Cearbhaill or Mac Cearbhaill, see Carroll .

variants.name: Carroll
meanings: Irish: shortened Anglicized form of Ó Cearbhaill or Mac Cearbhaill ‘descendant (or son) of Cearbhall’, a personal name perhaps based on cearbh ‘hacking’ and hence originally a byname for a butcher or a fierce warrior.
English and Scottish: variant of   Carrell  .

variants.name: Garfield
meanings: English (Midlands): topographic name from Middle English gor(e), gar(e) ‘triangular piece’ + feld ‘open country, field’ (Old English gār + feld), for someone who lived by a triangular piece of land.
English: alternatively, sometimes perhaps a variant of Carville (a name of Norman origin), with excrescent final -d.
Americanized form of one or more similar (like-sounding) Jewish surnames.

nameMeanings.name: William
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Probably the most successful of all the Old French names of Germanic origin that were introduced to England by the Normans. It is derived from Germanic wil ‘will, desire’ + helm ‘helmet, protection’. The fact that it was borne by the Conqueror himself does not seem to have inhibited its favour with the ‘conquered’ population: in the first century after the Conquest it was the commonest male name of all, and not only among the Normans. In the later Middle Ages it was overtaken by John , but continued to run second to that name until the 20th century, when the picture became more fragmented.

en.lifeSummary: When William de Camville was born about 1150, in Lilbourne, Northamptonshire, England, his father, Richard Lilbourne Stanton De Camville, was 36 and his mother, Melisende de Rethel, was 33. He had at least 3 sons with Aubreda Marmion. He died before 25 April 1200, in Clifton Campville, Staffordshire, England, United Kingdom.

ko.lifeSummary: William de Camville(이)가 대략 1150년 영국 노르섬프토실르 릴보우른에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Richard Lilbourne Stanton De Camville(은)는 36세였고, 그의 어머니 Melisende de Rethel(은)는 33세였다. 그는 Aubreda Marmion(와)과 적어도 세 명의 아들을 두었다. 그는 1200년 4월 25일 이전 영국 잉글랜드 스태퍼드실르 클리프턴 캠p빌에서 사망했다.
